page: Vultr vhf-1c-1gb is a virtual machine instance within the High Frequency Compute family. It features 1 shared vCPU based on the x86_64 architecture, utilizing an Intel Core Processor (Skylake, IBRS) clocked at 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 1.0 GB of system memory and 32 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. The instance is provisioned with a single complimentary public IPv4 address.

Benchmark data reveals a distinct performance profile. The single-core CPU performance, measured via stress-ng, registers at 1865.79, placing it in the average (middle 50%) category. However, because it is a single-core, shared-CPU instance, its multi-core stress-ng performance is identical at 1865.79, which falls into the bottom 10% tier. Memory bandwidth is limited, with read-only small block cached speeds at 132.54 GB/sec and large block uncached write speeds at 13.63 GB/sec, both ranking in the bottom 10%. Passmark CPU and memory scores also place the instance in the bottom 10% tier, with a CPU score of 2239.98 and a database operations score of 1027.68.

The primary tradeoff of the vhf-1c-1gb instance is its shared CPU allocation and low memory capacity, which constrains its throughput for concurrent or memory-heavy tasks. This is reflected in the Redis SET operations benchmark of 2.19 million operations per second and static web serving throughput of 1.94 GB/sec, both of which are in the bottom 10% tier. From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the instance offers a low-cost entry point for basic workloads, leveraging high-speed NVMe storage to offset some memory constraints, but it is not designed for resource-intensive applications.

This instance is best suited for lightweight, single-threaded workloads that do not require sustained high CPU utilization or large memory footprints. Appropriate use cases include basic web serving, small-scale development or testing environments, simple automation scripts, and lightweight utility servers.
description: Vultr vhf-1c-1gb is a virtual server in the High Frequency Compute family, configured with 1 shared vCPU on an Intel Skylake x86_64 architecture clocked at 2.0 GHz, 1.0 GB of RAM, and 32 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Performance benchmarks indicate average single-core CPU capabilities, with a stress-ng score of 1865.79. However, due to its single-core, shared-CPU design, multi-core and memory bandwidth metrics fall into the bottom 10% tier, with uncached large-block write speeds at 13.63 GB/sec. The instance includes one public IPv4 address and is qualitatively positioned as a budget-friendly option for low-resource tasks. It is best suited for lightweight workloads such as basic web serving, development testing, and simple background scripts that do not demand high memory or sustained multi-core processing.
